caveats: a: Occupation data are for NAICS 326200, not the narrowed six-digit population.
a: The excluded medical and consumer segments may have different administrative, marketing, and compliance task shares.

rho: Productivity and scrap savings are excluded unless they remove labor or avoid hiring.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat purchase orders, distributor/catalog sales, private-label programs, and project or supply contracts for industrial inputs and durable rubber products, with revenue primarily per unit, pound, sheet, or order.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 326299 is a residual category spanning industrial inputs and products, regulated contraceptive goods, and consumer novelties with materially different tasks, channels, and demand. The lens is narrowed to recurring B2B and government industrial/facility supply for a coherent screen; the excluded share is reflected in eligibility.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive units and shells; contraceptive devices, balloons, hair-care products, consumer novelties, and other predominantly branded consumer or regulated medical product models; tires, hose and belting, and molded mechanical goods classified elsewhere.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying industrial rubber tubing, reclaimed or ground-rubber inputs and products, rubber foam, mats, flooring, bumpers, pads, and similar non-tire, non-hose industrial or facility products to external business and government customers.

a.bridge: BLS publishes occupation structure for broader rubber-product manufacturing and NIST documents manufacturing AI uses in inspection, maintenance, forecasting, and process decisions. I map exposure to estimating, order entry, planning, documentation, purchasing, visual inspection, and analytic engineering/QA tasks, with limited exposure for hands-on compounding, extrusion, grinding, molding, cutting, testing, packing, and maintenance. The residual code's product diversity increases error in both directions, and small legacy plants may have less usable data than broader manufacturing.
caveats: Occupation data are for NAICS 326200, not the narrowed six-digit population.
The excluded medical and consumer segments may have different administrative, marketing, and compliance task shares.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task and payroll census across 15 independent firms covering tubing, foam, reclaimed rubber, mats, and molded facility products would materially change this range.
a.rationale: Digital commercial and planning work plus image-based quality tasks offer substitution, while the included businesses still require physical material conversion and handling.

d5.bridge: EPA documents established ground-rubber uses in asphalt and products including flooring, dock bumpers, livestock mats, roof pads, tiles, blocks, speed bumps, automotive parts, and plastics additives, and reports 220 million pounds used in asphalt rubber. I use the breadth of physical applications as evidence of a diversified demand floor, not as a current growth rate, then estimate constant-price five-year demand for the narrowed current basket. The archived EPA figures are old and may overstate or understate today's application mix.
caveats: EPA's archived statistics are dated and cover only the reclaimed/ground-rubber portion of the narrowed lens.
Demand varies materially among tubing, foam, recycled inputs, and finished facility products.
d5.change_evidence: Current inflation-adjusted Census product shipments, US Tire Manufacturers Association scrap-tire market reports, state DOT rubber-asphalt tonnage, and unit volumes from flooring, mat, tubing, and reclaimed-rubber suppliers would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Maintenance, construction, agriculture, industrial handling, and recycled-material demand support a stable-to-modestly-growing basket. Cyclical construction, health debates around some crumb-rubber uses, import competition, and product substitution create downside.

caveats: No firm-level product-mix census was found, so the share inside the narrowed lens is uncertain.
The provided count is estimated with a broad sector margin and may misclassify the EBITDA band.
e.change_evidence: Classify all 203 estimated in-band firms by product, customer channel, captive status, recurring external revenue, ownership, customer concentration, and normalized EBITDA using commercial data and direct calls.
e.rationale: The narrowed population excludes material consumer and medical business models inside this catch-all code. Among included firms, most repeat external B2B supply is transferable, but captive units, owner dependence, distress, customer concentration, and errors from the margin-bridged EBITDA count reduce eligibility further.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 326299 ($1,692,409,633) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 326299 ($13,001,788,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $370,284 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 326299); class <5: 131 firms, avg revenue/firm $673,206 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $98,625 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 71 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,931,155 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $282,914 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 73 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,593,110 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $526,391 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 133 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,726,759 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,717,970 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 70 firms, avg revenue/firm $40,333,029 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,908,789 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 67 firms, avg revenue/firm $121,361,463 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,779,454 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 203

caveats: An operator can remain necessary while production shifts offshore or consolidates into larger firms.
Environmental or health policy could eliminate specific recycled-rubber applications without eliminating the broader basket.
o.change_evidence: Buyer interviews and substitution studies across industrial tubing, facility products, rubberized asphalt, agriculture, and flooring would quantify self-service, insourcing, and material replacement.
o.rationale: The included basket consists of physical materials and durable goods that must be processed, tested, and delivered by an accountable manufacturer. Software may improve specification and ordering but cannot replace tubing, cushioning, flooring, bumpers, pads, or ground rubber; loss can arise from alternative materials, customer insourcing, and elimination of particular applications.

caveats: Capture differs widely between engineered tubing, reclaimed-rubber feedstock, and catalog facility products.
No representative contract dataset was found.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and five-year SKU-level price, rebate, freight, raw-material-index, volume, and gross-margin histories across each included product family would reveal pass-through.
q.rationale: Custom dimensions, compound know-how, freight economics, qualification, and recycled-content requirements can preserve some savings. Distributor comparison, bids, material-price indexing, and relatively standardized mats, flooring, tubing, and crumb rubber share benefits with customers over renewals.

rho.bridge: NIST reports 46% of manufacturers use AI tools and more than 80% expect greater use within two years, while listing data quality, initial cost, workforce skills, cybersecurity, and legacy integration barriers. I translate those broad indicators into five-year implementation of the exposed tasks. Inclusion of simple chatbots overstates operational implementation today; diffusion over five years offsets part of that bias.
caveats: General manufacturing adoption is not evidence of implementation in this residual industry.
Productivity and scrap savings are excluded unless they remove labor or avoid hiring.
rho.change_evidence: Comparable-plant deployment audits with baseline hours, equipment connectivity, validation results, sustained labor savings, and failed projects would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Office and forecasting tools can deploy quickly, but low-volume product variety, sparse defect labels, disconnected machines, environmental or product testing, and small-company IT capacity constrain factory implementation.

s5.bridge: Deloitte surveyed 500 US family-enterprise current- and next-generation members and found only 24% of the current generation strongly agreed its succession plan could withstand departure of an important family employee. I use this as evidence of broad succession pressure and reduce potential exits for family succession, non-control transactions, closures, and failed transfers. The survey is not industry-specific and does not measure timing; larger, better-advised firms may have greater readiness than this LMM population.
caveats: No denominator of eligible 326299 firms with qualifying transfers was found.
Succession fragility is not a sale probability.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and intended-exit interviews for the narrowed firm list plus five years of closed control deals from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, recycling-industry brokers, and rubber-product intermediaries would narrow the range.
s5.rationale: A fragmented set of industrial rubber processors should produce succession opportunities, but exact five-year control-transfer incidence is not observed and owner-dependent know-how can make a firm nontransferable.

principal_driver: Diversified recurring physical applications support durable operator-required demand.
principal_weakness: A catch-all industry code makes eligibility, task mix, commercial capture, and demand unusually uncertain.
